title: Rudnyckyj, DaromirSpiritual Economics: Islam, Globalization, and the Afterlife of Development2010Cornell University Press 289 pp. £60 (hardback) and £18.99 (paperback)
abstract: This is a strong ethnography of neoliberalism based on the observation of managers and employees in one of Indonesia's largest companies. Spiritual Economies advances one strand of the studies of transition to a market economy: the study of neoliberal subjectivity. The analyses are based on what could seem to be a counter-intuitive site (Karakatu Steel, a public enterprise), for neoliberalism is first and foremost associated with private business. However, Rudnyckyj underlines that neoliberal logic permeates the public as well as the private sector through transforming the conduct of managers and employees. Even though Karakatu Steel is one of the major public companies that are likely to be privatized, the book focuses on the willing change in daily conduct rather than the macro-pressures for privatization and deregulation. This is certainly not the first text to take this angle on market transition, but it is certainly one of the more interesting and empirically richer ones. The most appealing aspect of this book is the sustained description of training sessions that aim to instill market values through recourse to Islam. Spiritual Economies first puts these sessions in the context of modern Indonesian history. The shift from national developmentalism to a market-oriented understanding of the economy has paralleled the decline of a multicultural nationalism and the rise of Islamic identity. While more secular training programme have not raised a lot of interest, Islamic training sessions have spread throughout Indonesia. The book thoroughly focuses on the most popular of these Islamic neoliberal training programmes (Emotional and Spiritual Quotient, or ESQ). The author has participated in many of these sessions. The descriptions are quite vivid and written with an engaging prose. The quotes from the participants and organizers of these sessions also strengthen the overall picture. The reader can almost feel the energy of these sessions while reading the text. This superb ethnography, however, is clouded by a misguided discussion of Marxist critiques of neoliberalism. In today's social science and humanities, preconceived ‘theoretical’ notions have become the equivalent of ‘ideology’ in political culture and common sense. As one observer had noted long ago, ideology is always what ‘others’ have, but the common speaker always claims and thinks that s/he is outside (or even above) ideology. Likewise, especially anti-Marxists blame Marxist or Marxian social scientists of imposing their theoretical stance on empirical material. The assumption, it seems, is that the ‘objective’ social scientist is free from this ‘flaw’. Or put differently, there is a pre-existing reality (p. 16), independent from the ‘mind of the analyst’ (p. 20), and the goal of social science is to grasp this reality. Rudnyckyj accuses Marxism-inspired works on neoliberalism of focusing on aspects of neoliberalism that have little resonance in ‘reality’. Spiritual Economies rather uses neoliberalism as an ‘analytical category that refers to a specific type of rationality … a practical method for organizing human conduct’ (p. 21). In other words, we are led to believe that the study of rationality and conduct is more loyal to empirical reality than the study of the subordination of labour. Yet, as the author recognizes, this focus builds on an established theoretical stance, that of Foucault (and in Rudnyckyj's case, a Foucault-inspired reading of Weber too). The rest of the book is also thoroughly Foucauldian, with abundant reference to Foucault and his followers. The text, therefore, would have benefited from framing the tension with Marxian analyses of neoliberalism as a rivalry between two theories, rather than positing an incompatibility between the theoretician's mind and empirical reality. Then we could ask which of the two approaches is more productive as a research programme. Even this, however, might not be the best way of approaching the phenomenon of neoliberalism, since the subordination of labour and the willing transformation of conduct might be the two faces of the same coin rather than necessarily competing explanations. Most, though not all, Marxist scholars have a tendency to overplay the struggles and resistances that neoliberalism generates. Spiritual Economies (along with other Foucauldian studies of market transition) is a must reading to avoid such too-hopeful critique. In that sense, the book at hand should be seen as of relevance not only to anthropologists but to anybody interested in neoliberalism. Yet, most Foucauldians have the reverse tendency: imagining too seamless a Panopticon. Rudnyckyj's descriptions verge on a suffocating dystopia, where even workers’ struggles can only end up reinforcing neoliberal values (Chapter 6). One would expect (and there is much research to corroborate the expectation) that religion, religious employees, and actors of other colours might operate counter to neoliberal desire at different times and in different places. Exclusively Foucauldian texts might notice instances of the questioning of neoliberalism (pp. 66–9, 111–12, 154), but would not be able to sufficiently theorize them. A research programme that integrates nodes of neoliberal panopticism as well as nodes of anti-neoliberal struggle (with an explicit exploration of when, where and why one predominates) could lead to richer empirical and theoretical results.
